channel.title: Vendor Management on Runink
channel.description: Recent content in Vendor Management on Runink

item.title: How to Write a Logistics RFP That Actually Gets You the Right Partner
item.description:
What are the Key Takeaways from this Executive Summary?

Quick Answer: Most logistics RFPs fail not because the market lacks capable providers, but because the RFP itself is poorly scoped, over-indexed on price, and silent on the technology integrations and SLA frameworks that determine long-term partnership success. A structured RFP lifecycle — from needs assessment through contract negotiation — dramatically improves vendor alignment, reduces transition risk, and protects OTIF and fill rate performance during onboarding.
  • A vague scope is the most expensive mistake in logistics procurement — it invites misaligned proposals, inflated pricing, and months of post-award renegotiation.
  • Great RFPs balance cost structure transparency (cost-plus, fixed, gain-share) with measurable SLA/KPI frameworks that include enforceable penalties and incentives.
  • AI-powered platforms like Runink can pre-populate RFP data from live operations, benchmark carrier responses against market rates, and score proposals objectively — removing bias and compressing evaluation timelines.

Why Do Most Logistics RFPs Fail Before a Single Proposal Arrives?

Quick Answer: The majority of logistics RFPs fail because they are drafted in isolation from actual operational data. Procurement teams rely on stale volume forecasts, omit lane-level detail, and ask 200 generic questions that no provider reads carefully — resulting in boilerplate responses that make meaningful comparison impossible.

The 3PL market in North America alone exceeds $300 billion, according to Armstrong & Associates. With that much spend at stake, you would expect rigorous procurement discipline. Instead, the typical logistics RFP suffers from a predictable set of failures:

item.title: Carrier Scorecards — How to Hold Your Transportation Partners Accountable with Data
item.description:
What are the Key Takeaways from this Executive Summary?

Quick Answer: A carrier scorecard is a systematic, data-driven tool that measures and compares carrier performance across standardized KPIs — replacing gut feelings and tribal knowledge with objective, repeatable evaluations. Shippers who implement structured scorecards gain leverage during rate negotiations, reduce service failures, and build carrier relationships grounded in mutual accountability rather than anecdotal feedback.
  • Most shippers manage dozens of carriers without a structured evaluation framework. The result is that underperformers persist, top carriers are under-rewarded, and freight decisions are driven by relationships instead of results.
  • Effective scorecards track weighted KPIs — on-time delivery, tender acceptance, claims ratio, billing accuracy — scored and reviewed on a defined cadence. Not all metrics carry equal weight, and the scoring methodology must reflect what actually matters to your network.
  • Carriers prioritize shippers who measure and communicate performance. A transparent scorecard program signals operational maturity and gives carriers clear targets — creating a competitive dynamic that lifts service levels across your entire portfolio.

What Is a Carrier Scorecard and Why Do Most Shippers Still Lack One?

Quick Answer: A carrier scorecard is a standardized performance report that evaluates each transportation partner against a consistent set of KPIs — on-time delivery, claims ratio, tender acceptance, billing accuracy, and more — scored, weighted, and reviewed on a recurring cadence to drive accountability and inform procurement decisions.

If you manage 30, 50, or 100+ carriers, you already know the problem. The account manager at your third-largest LTL provider insists their service is excellent. Your dock supervisors disagree. Your AP team flags billing discrepancies every cycle. And your customer service group is fielding complaints about late deliveries on a lane you thought was covered. Everyone has an opinion. Nobody has data.
